settings.question: What is Malassezia and why does it matter for skincare?
settings.answer: Malassezia is a genus of yeast that naturally lives on human skin. When it overgrows, it can cause fungal acne (Malassezia folliculitis), seborrheic dermatitis, dandruff, and tinea versicolor. A lot of common skincare ingredients contain fatty acids in the C12 to C24 range that this yeast feeds on, which tends to make those conditions flare. This tool flags those ingredients on a label.

settings.question: How do I check if my skincare is fungal acne safe?
settings.answer: Use this free tool. Paste in the product's ingredient list, scan the label with your camera, or pick a product from the built-in database of 77+ popular items (CeraVe, Vanicream, The Ordinary, La Roche-Posay, and so on). Every Malassezia-feeding ingredient gets flagged with a swap suggestion. There's more background in our Malassezia Handbook.

settings.question: What ingredients should I avoid for fungal acne?
settings.answer: Avoid ingredients with fatty acid chains in the C12 to C24 range. That covers most plant oils (coconut, olive, argan, jojoba, rosehip), free fatty acids (lauric, stearic, oleic), esters such as isopropyl myristate and glyceryl stearate, polysorbates 20/40/60/80, and fermented ingredients like galactomyces ferment filtrate. Reach for squalane, MCT oil (C8/C10 only), mineral oil, dimethicone, or caprylic/capric triglyceride instead.

settings.question: Is coconut oil safe for fungal acne?
settings.answer: No. Coconut oil is loaded with lauric acid (C12), myristic acid (C14), and palmitic acid (C16), which Malassezia feeds on directly. Use MCT oil (caprylic/capric triglyceride, C8/C10 only) or squalane instead.

settings.question: What moisturizer is safe for seborrheic dermatitis?
settings.answer: Look for moisturizers with no fatty acids in the C12 to C24 range and no plant oils. Good bases are squalane, dimethicone, petrolatum, or caprylic/capric triglyceride. Commonly recommended seb derm options include Vanicream products, Sebamed Clear Face Care Gel, and The Ordinary Natural Moisturizing Factors. Run any product through the checker above if you're not sure.

settings.question: Is CeraVe safe for fungal acne?
settings.answer: Depends on the product. CeraVe Foaming Facial Cleanser scores well and has only one flagged ingredient. CeraVe Moisturizing Cream contains cetearyl alcohol and cetyl alcohol (debated fatty alcohols); some people with fungal acne tolerate them, others don't. Check specific CeraVe products in the tool above.

settings.question: What skincare ingredients are safe for tinea versicolor?
settings.answer: Tinea versicolor is caused by the same Malassezia yeast behind fungal acne and seb derm. Safe ingredients include glycerin, hyaluronic acid, niacinamide, squalane, dimethicone, zinc pyrithione, ketoconazole, selenium sulfide, salicylic acid, and azelaic acid. Skip coconut oil, olive oil, shea butter, and any other plant oil with C12 to C24 fatty acids.

settings.question: Are fatty alcohols safe for fungal acne?
settings.answer: Fatty alcohols like cetyl alcohol (C16), stearyl alcohol (C18), and cetearyl alcohol are debated in the fungal acne community. They tend to be better tolerated than oils and esters, but some people with Malassezia-prone skin still react. The tool marks these as "caution" rather than "unsafe." Patch test first if you're not sure.

settings.question: What sunscreen is safe for fungal acne?
settings.answer: Look for mineral sunscreens with zinc oxide or titanium dioxide as the active, and check that the inactive list is free of plant oils and fatty acid esters. EltaMD UV Clear SPF 46 and CeraVe Mineral Body Sunscreen SPF 50 are popular picks. Run the full ingredient list through the tool; plenty of sunscreens hide problem ingredients in the inactive section.

settings.question: Is this tool a substitute for seeing a dermatologist?
settings.answer: No. This tool is educational and helps you screen ingredients. It isn't a diagnosis or medical advice. If you think you have fungal acne, seborrheic dermatitis, tinea versicolor, or another Malassezia-related condition, see a dermatologist for diagnosis and treatment, or book a video consultation with one of our licensed providers.
